votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking the exact 10-character format, parsing the digits, and validating the month and day ranges.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq specification, correctly parsing the date string, checking the separators, and validating the month and day ranges according to the same rules.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, translating the string parsing, digit conversion, and date validation logic exactly as defined in Coq.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks for exactly 10 characters, verifies separators are '-', parses month/day/year as 2/2/4 digits respectively, validates month is 1-12, and validates day is between 1 and the maximum days for that month (31 for months 1,3,5,7,8,10,12; 30 for months 4,6,9,11; 29 for month 2). The Python returns True/False matching when the Coq Prop would be True/False for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks for exactly 10 characters, verifies separators are '-', parses month/day/year as 2/2/4 digits respectively, validates month is 1-12, and validates day is between 1 and the maximum days for that month (31 for months 1,3,5,7,8,10,12; 30 for 4,6,9,11; 29 for February). The Coq spec returns a Prop (True/False) and the Python returns a boolean with the same logic. All concrete inputs will produce matching results.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks for exactly 10 characters, verifies separators are '-', parses month/day/year as 2/2/4 digits respectively, validates month is 1-12, and validates day is between 1 and the maximum days for that month (31 for months 1,3,5,7,8,10,12; 30 for months 4,6,9,11; 29 for month 2). The Python returns True/False matching when the Coq Prop would be True/False for all concrete inputs.

Both implementations accept exactly 10-character strings in mm-dd-yyyy format with hyphens, require month 01-12, day within 1..days_in_month(month) (with Feb allowed up to 29), and require all month/day/year characters to be digits. The Python helpers mirror the Coq parsing and day limits, and the preconditions match.

Both check exact mm-dd-yyyy length 10 with '-' separators, parse month/day/year as digits, require 1<=month<=12 and 1<=day<=days_in_month(month) with Feb=29, and otherwise reject. Year is only required to be four digits. Behavior matches on all inputs.

Both implementations require exactly the mm-dd-yyyy format with '-' separators, parse digits identically, enforce month in 1..12 and day within days_in_month (Feb up to 29), and only check that the year is four digits without further constraints. The Python helpers mirror the Coq digit-to-nat conversions, so concrete judgments align.
